content:
# Acts Chapter 5 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 230-231 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_005_page_001.jpg, ACT_005_page_002.jpg

---

**1** But a certain man named Ananias, with Sapphira his wife, sold a piece of property, **2** and kept back part of the price for himself, his wife also being aware of it, and brought a certain portion and laid it at the feet of the apostles. **3** But Peter said, "Ananias, why has Satan filled your heart to lie to the Holy Spirit and to keep back part of the price of the land? **4** While it remained unsold, did it not remain your own? And after it was sold, was it not at your disposal? How is it that you have conceived this deed in your heart? You have not lied to men but to God." **5** And when Ananias heard these words, he fell down and breathed his last. And great fear came upon all who heard of it. **6** And the young men arose, wrapped him up, and carried him out, and buried him.

**7** And it came to pass, after an interval of about three hours, that his wife came in, not knowing what had happened. **8** And Peter responded to her, "Tell me whether you sold the land for this much?" And she said, "Yes, for that much." **9** And Peter said to her, "How is it that you have agreed together to test the Spirit of the Lord? Behold, the feet of those who have buried your husband are at the door, and they will carry you out." **10** And she fell down immediately at his feet and breathed her last. And the young men came in and found her dead, and they carried her out and buried her beside her husband. **11** And great fear came upon the whole church and upon all who heard of these things.

**12** Now by the hands of the apostles many signs and wonders were being done among the people. And they were all with one accord in Solomon's Portico. **13** But none of the rest dared to join them, yet the people held them in high esteem. **14** And all the more believers in the Lord were added, multitudes of both men and women, **15** so that they even carried the sick out into the streets and laid them on cots and mats, that as Peter came by, at least his shadow might fall on some of them. **16** And the multitude from the cities surrounding Jerusalem also gathered, bringing the sick and those afflicted by unclean spirits, and they were all being healed.

**17** But the high priest rose up, and all who were with him (that is, the sect of the Sadducees), and they were filled with jealousy, **18** and they laid hands on the apostles and put them in the public prison. **19** But an angel of the Lord during the night opened the doors of the prison and brought them out and said, **20** "Go, stand in the temple and speak to the people all the words of this Life." **21** And when they heard this, they entered the temple at daybreak and began to teach.

Now when the high priest and those with him arrived, they called together the council, even the whole senate of the sons of Israel, and sent to the prison to have them brought. **22** But when the officers came, they did not find them in the prison, and they returned and reported, **23** saying, "We found the prison shut and locked with all security, and the guards standing at the doors, but when we opened them, we found no one inside." **24** Now when the captain of the temple and the chief priests heard these words, they were greatly perplexed about them, wondering what this might become.

**25** Then someone came and reported to them, "Behold, the men whom you put in prison are standing in the temple and teaching the people." **26** Then the captain went with the officers and brought them, but without violence, for they were afraid of the people, lest they be stoned.

**27** And when they had brought them, they set them before the council. And the high priest questioned them, **28** saying, "We strictly commanded you not to teach in this name, and behold, you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching, and you intend to bring this man's blood upon us." **29** But Peter and the apostles answered and said, "We must obey God rather than men. **30** The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom you killed by hanging him on a tree. **31** God exalted him at his right hand as Leader and Savior, to give repentance to Israel and forgiveness of sins. **32** And we are witnesses of these things, and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey him."

**33** And when they heard this, they were enraged and wanted to put them to death. **34** But a certain Pharisee in the council named Gamaliel, a teacher of the law held in honor by all the people, stood up and ordered the men to be put outside for a short time. **35** And he said to them, "Men of Israel, take care what you are about to do with these men. **36** For before these days Theudas rose up, claiming to be somebody, and a number of men, about four hundred, joined him. He was put to death, and all who followed him were dispersed and came to nothing. **37** After him, Judas the Galilean rose up in the days of the census and drew away people after him. He also perished, and all who followed him were scattered.

**38** "And now I say to you, keep away from these men and let them alone, for if this plan or this work is of men, it will be overthrown; **39** but if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow them — lest you even be found fighting against God." And they were persuaded by him. **40** And they called in the apostles and beat them and charged them not to speak in the name of Jesus, and released them.

**41** So they went out from the presence of the council, rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for the sake of the Name. **42** And every day, in the temple and from house to house, they did not cease teaching and proclaiming the good news that the Christ is Jesus.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"breathed his last" (vv. 5, 10):** Greek *exepsyxen* (ἐξέψυξεν) — literally "breathed out [his soul]." This formal term for death appears in the New Testament only in Acts (here and at 12:23). Retained for its gravity and precision.

2. **"church" (v. 11):** Greek *ekklesia* (ἐκκλησία). This is the first occurrence of the word "church" in Acts, marking a significant terminological development. The Sinaiticus reading is consistent with the broader manuscript tradition here.

3. **"this Life" (v. 20):** Greek *tes zoes tautes* (τῆς ζωῆς ταύτης). The angel's instruction to speak "all the words of this Life" is striking — "Life" is capitalized here as it carries theological weight, referring not merely to biological existence but to the message of eternal life in Christ.

4. **"Leader and Savior" (v. 31):** Greek *archegon kai sotera* (ἀρχηγὸν καὶ σωτῆρα). *Archegos* can mean "author," "pioneer," "prince," or "leader." "Leader" is chosen as a formal equivalent that preserves the active, authoritative sense without over-specifying. The same term was used in Acts 3:15 ("Author of life"); context here favors "Leader" in its pairing with "Savior."

5. **"hanging him on a tree" (v. 30):** Greek *kremasantes epi xylou* (κρεμάσαντες ἐπὶ ξύλου). This deliberately echoes Deuteronomy 21:22-23 (LXX), linking the crucifixion to the Torah's language of cursed death. "Tree" (*xylon*) rather than "cross" (*stauros*) is maintained as the formal equivalent.

6. **"the Name" (v. 41):** Greek *tou onomatos* (τοῦ ὀνόματος). The Sinaiticus text reads simply "the Name" without the later addition "of Jesus" found in some manuscripts. This absolute usage — "the Name" standing alone — reflects early Christian reverence and is preserved as found in the manuscript.

7. **"fighting against God" (v. 39):** Greek *theomachoi* (θεομάχοι) — a compound meaning "God-fighters." Rendered as "fighting against God" to preserve the verbal force of Gamaliel's warning.

8. **"senate" (v. 21):** Greek *gerousian* (γερουσίαν). This term, distinct from *synedrion* (council/Sanhedrin), refers to the assembly of elders. It may be synonymous with the Sanhedrin or designate an expanded body. Retained as "senate" for formal precision.

9. **Sinaiticus variants:** The Sinaiticus text at v. 28 reads the indirect command form ("we strictly commanded you") consistent with the majority of witnesses. At v. 34, Sinaiticus names Gamaliel without the later gloss "a Pharisee" being contested; the reading is stable here.

— *LOGOS*
